title: From Invisible to Unstoppable: How Women Leaders Can Transform Burnout into Purpose
excerpt: In this transformative solo episode, Aimee Rickabus reveals why your invisible labor is actually world-shaping leadership and shares how to reclaim your power through purpose. A raw, honest conversation for women who are holding everything together behind the scenes.
metaDescription: Discover how to transform invisible labor into leadership power. CEO Aimee Rickabus shares the one-degree shift that changes everything for overwhelmed women.
keyTakeaways: Invisible labor performed by women is actually a form of world-shaping leadership that deserves recognition and honor.
Burnout is not a personal failure but a cultural symptom of systems that don't value women's contributions.
You can reclaim your purpose through small one-degree shifts rather than dramatic life overhauls.
Domestic diplomacy, ritual creation, and sisterhood are revolutionary acts of leadership.
You don't need credentials or permission to lead - you already have the spark within you.
Presence matters more than five-year plans when it comes to creating meaningful change.
Every woman has the power to turn pain into purpose and become a changemaker right where she is.

pullQuotes.text: You don't need credentials to lead. You need love.
Burnout is not a failure, but a cultural symptom
You don't need permission — you already have the spark.

content:
There's something profoundly revolutionary happening in kitchens, boardrooms, and living rooms across the world. It's happening in the quiet moments between meetings, in the careful orchestration of family schedules, and in the countless acts of care that keep our communities functioning. It's the invisible labor that women perform every single day, and it's time we recognized it for what it truly is: world-shaping leadership.

In this deeply personal episode of The Manage Her, Aimee Rickabus pulls back the curtain on a conversation that's long overdue. This isn't just another discussion about work-life balance or productivity hacks. This is a love letter to the women who are holding everything together behind the scenes, the quiet leaders who are moving the world forward one small act at a time.

The Truth About Invisible Labor

You know that feeling when you're managing seventeen different moving pieces while everyone else seems to glide through their day? When you're the one remembering the birthdays, coordinating the carpools, mediating the conflicts, and somehow still expected to show up as your best self in every other area of your life? That's not just busy work. That's leadership in its purest form.

Aimee challenges us to reframe the narrative around the emotional and logistical labor that predominantly falls on women's shoulders. Instead of viewing these responsibilities as burdens that hold us back from our "real" work, what if we recognized them as evidence of our natural leadership abilities? The woman who can navigate family dynamics while building a business isn't doing too much - she's demonstrating the kind of multifaceted leadership our world desperately needs.

This reframe isn't about adding more to your plate or glorifying overwhelm. It's about giving yourself credit for the incredible work you're already doing and understanding that your ability to see, manage, and care for multiple layers of complexity is a superpower, not a weakness.

The One-Degree Shift That Changes Everything

Here's where the conversation gets really practical. Aimee introduces the concept of the "one-degree shift" - the idea that massive transformation doesn't require massive disruption. Instead of waiting for the perfect moment to make sweeping changes to your life, what if you started with the smallest possible adjustment that moves you toward alignment?

This approach is particularly powerful for women who feel trapped in their current circumstances or overwhelmed by the gap between where they are and where they want to be. The one-degree shift acknowledges your current reality while creating space for growth. It might look like having one difficult conversation you've been avoiding, setting one boundary that protects your energy, or taking one small step toward a dream that's been calling you.

The beauty of this approach is that it works with your life, not against it. You don't need to quit your job, move across the country, or completely reinvent yourself overnight. You just need to be willing to make one tiny adjustment that honors both where you are and where you're going.

Revolutionary Acts in Disguise

One of the most powerful themes in this episode is the recognition that what we often dismiss as mundane or ordinary is actually revolutionary. Aimee talks about domestic diplomacy - the art of creating harmony in your home environment - as a form of leadership that translates directly to every other area of life. When you're negotiating bedtimes, managing competing schedules, and creating systems that work for your family, you're developing skills that Fortune 500 CEOs pay consultants millions of dollars to learn.

The same is true for the rituals you create and the communities you build. Every time you show up consistently for the people in your life, every time you create a sense of belonging for others, every time you hold space for someone else's growth, you're practicing leadership at the highest level. These aren't consolation prizes for women who aren't doing "real" work - they're the fundamental building blocks of healthy organizations, communities, and societies.

Sisterhood as Strategy

Perhaps one of the most important messages in this episode is the reminder that you don't have to figure this out alone. The pressure to be everything to everyone while maintaining your own sense of identity and purpose is not a burden you're meant to carry in isolation. There's profound power in connecting with other women who understand the particular challenges and opportunities of female leadership.

This isn't about commiseration or complaint sessions. It's about strategic sisterhood - the intentional cultivation of relationships with women who can see your blind spots, celebrate your wins, and remind you of your power when you forget. These connections become the foundation from which you can take bigger risks, dream bigger dreams, and create the kind of change that aligns with your deepest values.

Your Spark is Already Enough

If you take only one thing from this episode, let it be this: you don't need permission to start leading from wherever you are right now. You don't need more credentials, more confidence, or more clarity about your five-year plan. You need presence. You need to trust the spark that's already within you and give yourself permission to act on it.

This message hits differently when you're in the thick of feeling invisible or undervalued. It's easy to believe that leadership happens somewhere else, by someone else, with resources you don't have access to. But the truth is that every woman who has ever created meaningful change started exactly where you are now - with a spark of possibility and the courage to take one small step forward.

The world needs what you have to offer, exactly as you are, right where you are. Your unique combination of experiences, insights, and care is not just valuable - it's irreplaceable. And the time to step into that power isn't someday when everything aligns perfectly. The time is now, with whatever you have, wherever you are.

This episode is ultimately an invitation - an invitation to see yourself differently, to value your contributions more fully, and to step into your power with intention and purpose. You're not just managing your life. You're managing change, managing growth, and managing the kind of leadership our world desperately needs. And that makes you not just busy, not just overwhelmed, but absolutely unstoppable.

quiz.title: What's Your Invisible Leadership Style?
quiz.description: You're already leading in ways you might not recognize. Discover how your unique approach to life's challenges reveals your natural leadership superpower.

questions.question: When your family or team is facing a stressful situation, what's your natural response?

options.text: I start mentally organizing and coordinating what needs to happen to fix it
I look for small, manageable steps we can take to move forward
I focus on making sure everyone feels heard and supported first
I dig deep to find the lesson or opportunity hidden in the challenge

questions.question: You're feeling overwhelmed by your daily responsibilities. How do you typically handle it?

options.text: I create systems and lists to manage everything more efficiently
I pick one small thing to change that might make everything easier
I reach out to my support network and focus on connection
I remind myself that this difficult season will teach me something important

questions.question: What gives you the most energy in your leadership role, whether at work or home?

options.text: Seeing all the pieces come together perfectly because of your planning
Noticing how small improvements create positive ripple effects
Watching others grow and succeed because you believed in them
Knowing you're building something meaningful from difficult circumstances

questions.question: When someone asks how you manage to handle so much, you usually think:

options.text: If I don't keep track of everything, who will? Someone has to see the whole picture
I just focus on the next right step instead of trying to do everything at once
I couldn't do it without my people - we all support each other
Every challenge I've faced has prepared me for what I'm handling now

questions.question: Your ideal way to create change in the world would be:

options.text: Behind the scenes, making sure all the important details are handled
Through consistent, small actions that inspire others to do the same
By creating spaces where people feel safe to be authentic and grow
By sharing your story and showing others what's possible

questions.question: When you think about your biggest accomplishments, they usually involve:

options.text: Successfully managing complex situations that others found overwhelming
Gradual improvements that made a real difference over time
Helping others discover their potential and supporting their success
Overcoming obstacles that seemed impossible and proving it could be done

results.title: The Quiet Orchestrator
results.description: You see the big picture and naturally coordinate all the moving pieces behind the scenes. Your leadership shows up in how you create harmony and keep everything flowing smoothly, even when others don't notice the complexity you're managing. You have an incredible gift for making the impossible look effortless. This episode will help you recognize the profound impact of your invisible labor and show you how to honor this leadership style without burning out.

results.title: The Gentle Revolutionary
results.description: You lead through small, meaningful changes that ripple outward in powerful ways. While others chase dramatic transformations, you understand that real change happens through consistent, intentional shifts. Your superpower lies in creating safe spaces where others can grow and flourish. This episode will validate your approach and show you how those one-degree shifts you're already making are actually revolutionary acts of leadership.

results.title: The Intuitive Connector
results.description: Your leadership flows from your ability to sense what others need and create the connections that make communities thrive. You're the one people turn to, the bridge-builder, the heart of your circles. You lead with empathy and presence, understanding that relationships are the foundation of all meaningful change. This episode will help you see how your natural ability to hold space for others is a profound form of world-shaping leadership.

results.title: The Purpose-Driven Transformer
results.description: You have an innate ability to turn challenges into opportunities and pain into purpose. Your leadership emerges from your willingness to face difficulties head-on and transform them into something meaningful. You inspire others through your resilience and your refusal to let setbacks define you. This episode will speak directly to your soul, showing you how to channel your transformative energy without depleting yourself in the process.

transcript: [Music] Hi everyone and welcome back to the manage her the podcast where we shine a light on the invisible work of women and reframe it as leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickabus, CEO of Tomahawk information solutions, mama of six and author of the manager unveiling women's invisible work and sparking a leadership revolution. And today I want to have a deeper conversation with you. Just us. No guests, no small talk, just real talk. I believe now more than ever that every woman carries something powerful inside her. A spark, a purpose, a quiet fire. And sometimes we don't even know it's there until life presses on us and we realize this matters to me. I was made for something more than surviving. So today we're going to talk about why the invisible work you do every day is a form of worldchanging leadership. How to find your spark even if you're exhausted, overwhelmed, or unsure. What your purpose might actually look like in real life, not Instagram life. And how tiny acts rooted in love can ripple out farther than you'll ever see. This is for the moms who haven't slept through the night in years. This is for the women building empires from their phones in between laundry loads. This is for the caretakers, the change makers, the ones who wonder if what they do really matters. Let me say this loud and clear. It does. Chapter one, the invisible work that runs the world. Let's start here. Let's name the truth. Most of the work that women do in the world, it's invisible. And in the manager, I call this the shadow economy of women. It's the work that's done off the clock outside of the spreadsheet behind the curtain. It's the meals made, the school form signed, the mental health management, the silent prayers whispered while rocking a crying child. It's caregiving. It's emotional labor. It's the work of seeing and being for everyone else. And for decades, even centuries, we've been conditioned to believe that this labor is secondary, supportive, soft. But if you've ever managed a family calendar, navigated a health crisis, or kept your household afloat during an economic uncertainty, you know it's anything but soft. It's strategic. It's adaptive. It's powerful. It's what I call domestic executive functioning. And here's what I realized. The skill set that helps us thrive in the home is the same skill set that's needed to run companies, lead teams, and build movements. We've just never been told that those talents count. That's what the manager is all about. It's about making the invisible visible. It's about drawing a direct line between your unpaid labor and your leadership potential. Because the minute we reframe the work, we reclaim the power. Now, I want to talk about the idea of the onederee shift. We get stuck thinking that if we're going to find our purpose, we need to take a giant leap. Quit our jobs, move to another country, start a 501c3. But most of the time, the path to purpose is much quieter. It starts with a whisper, not a roar. Maybe you've been through something. Maybe you've seen something you can't unsee. Maybe your own pain has become a doorway into compassion. I want to share something I didn't include in my book, a story from a mom in our community. She went through a traumatic birth with her second child. It left her physically drained and emotionally shattered. But months months later, once she healed, she started showing up at the hospital once a week just to sit in the NICU and hold other people's babies who didn't have family present. That's it. No big announcement, no fundraising, just love. That is leadership. That is purpose. That is how we move the world forward, one degree at a time. And here's the magic. When you take that one small step, doors start to open. You meet people, you learn things, you find that you're not alone. That spark becomes a calling and that calling becomes a contribution. Let's talk about careers and callings and how they don't have to be separate. In the manager, I share stories of women who turned their personal struggles into professional pivots. One woman, a former makeup artist, had a child with oral motor issues. She was spending months researching, advocating, and eventually became a certified myofunctional therapist. Today, she runs a thriving practice and helps families just like hers. This didn't start as a business plan. It started with a need. It started with a mom who refused to give up. I want you to hear this. If you're in a season of survival right now, you don't need a 5-year plan. You need presence. Stay present to your life. Stay open to the moments that make your heart burn and your voice shake. Your career can evolve out of your calling, but only if you give yourself permission to care deeply, to follow curiosity, to explore what you might be good at, even if you've never done it before. We all start somewhere, usually somewhere messy. I've explored how leadership doesn't always look like a title. Sometimes it looks like a meal dropped off at the at the right moment, a Google search in the middle of the night, a question asked with love. And most importantly, we unpack the idea that our unpaid labor is not just valuable, it's vital. Today, I want to take that foundation and go even deeper. We're going to talk about burnout and boundary setting and how we can reframe rest as a responsibility, the power of community rituals and why tradition is a revolutionary act. How our personal healing creates global ripple effects, and what it really means to be a change maker in today's world. even if you feel like a hot mess most days. Let's start with burnout. I don't know how a single woman who hasn't walked the edge of exhaustion or fallen straight into the fire of it. In the manager, I have a whole section dedicated to this. It's not labeled burnout, but it's everywhere. It's in the overwhelm, the multitasking, the martyrdom, the invisible expectations that pile onto our shoulders until we're crushed by the weight of trying to be everything to everyone. Here's the truth. Burnout is not a personal failure. It is a cultural consequence of chronic overexhaustion and chronic underrecognition. And yet, we're still made to feel like we should just do better, meal prep more, meditate more, manage our time better. But burnout doesn't get solved by doing more. It gets solved by getting seen. When you feel seen, truly, deeply recognized. Something shifts inside of you. You remember who you are beneath the performance. You remember that rest is not a luxury. It's part of the job. So if you're listening to this and you're running on empty, this is your permission slip to do the one thing that replenishes your soul. Maybe that's a walk alone, a nap in the middle of the day, or just saying no to something that's good but not aligned. As I wrote in the book, you are not here to be consumed. You are here to create and to create to really lead you have to protect your energy because your life depends on it. Let's talk about something that often gets overlooked in leadership conversation and that's tradition. One of the most beautiful parts of my own life has been the seaside sisterhood. The community of women who surround me in my neighborhood. We have traditions. The bunny stomp, Christmas carolling, chili cookoffs, Golden Girls bunko. These aren't just fun events. They're anchors. They're culture keepers. They are moments that build belonging. They are how we mark time and say to one another, "You're not alone." And here's what I believe. Ritual is resistance in a world that's constantly trying to isolate us, rush us, divide us. Gathering with intention is a radical act. Saying we will show up for each other, even if it's just once a month, over themed cocktails and laughter. It's how we heal the collective nervous system. So if you've ever been the one to organize, plan, cook, decorate, or simply invite, you are doing holy work. You are the glue. And the glue is leadership. In the manager, I call this domestic diplomacy. The unseen work of weaving people together, of creating safe spaces, of holding joy even when life is heavy. So let's stop minimizing it. Let's honor it. One of the things I say over and over in my book is the most powerful revolutions start in the smallest rooms. Your living room, your child's hospital room, your classroom where you volunteer, the break room where you ask someone how they're really doing. When you heal something in yourself, when you answer a call, when you move toward the pain instead of away from it, you create a ripple and that ripple can turn into a wave. Some of the guests we'll be featuring on this podcast have taken their own pain and turned it into global action. Women who work with organizations rescuing children from sex trafficking. Mothers who became practitioners after navigating rare medical issues with their children. Leaders who coach women through hormone health, burnout, and identity loss. Healers, artists, entrepreneurs, caregivers, all of them driven by spark and service. The mission is not about being everything. It's about choosing one thing. one thing that lights you up and stepping towards it. That's how we move the world forward. Let me close with this. You are a change maker. Whether you're leading a Fortune 500 company or a Girl Scout troop, you are shaping culture. Whether you're donating $10 a month or starting a nonprofit, you are investing in the future. Whether you're posting resources on Instagram or quietly helping your neighbor, you are standing for something. You don't need credentials to make a difference. You don't need a perfect life to start helping others. You don't even need clarity. You just need to be willing. Willing to move one degree. Willing to trust your life. Willing to believe that your story matters and that someone else is waiting for your voice. So I'll ask you again. What is your one thing? What's that spark? What's the shift? What's the next faithful step you could take? Take it today. quietly, gently, boldly, because that's how we change the world. Thank you for being with me for this very personal episode of the manager. If this message resonates with you, share it with a friend, leave a review, or better yet, let it move you into action. You are not behind. You are being prepared. I'll see you next time. [Music]
